paper_title: Proactive Agent Research Environment: Simulating Active Users to Evaluate Proactive Assistants

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Proactive agents that anticipate user needs and autonomously execute tasks hold great promise as digital assistants, yet the lack of realistic user simulation frameworks hinders their development. Existing approaches model apps as flat tool-calling APIs, failing to capture the stateful and sequential nature of user interaction in digital environments and making realistic user simulation infeasible. We introduce Proactive Agent Research Environment (Pare), a framework for building and evaluating proactive agents in digital environments. Pare models applications as finite state machines with stateful navigation and state-dependent action space for the user simulator, enabling active user simulation. Building on this foundation, we present Pare-Bench, a benchmark of 143 diverse tasks spanning communication, productivity, scheduling, and lifestyle apps, designed to test context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration.

Existing approaches model apps as flat tool-calling APIs, failing to capture the stateful and sequential nature of user interaction in digital environments and making realistic user simulation infeasible. We introduce Proactive Agent Research Environment (Pare), framework for building and evaluating proactive agents in digital environments. Pare models applications as finite state machines with stateful navigation and state-dependent action space for the user simulator, enabling active user simulation. Building on this foundation, we present Pare-Bench, benchmark of 143 diverse tasks spanning communication, productivity, scheduling, and lifestyle apps, designed to test context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration. Project Page: https://github.com/deepakn97/pare # Correspondence: dnathani@ucsb.edu, ericxwang@ucsb.edu 1. Introduction Autonomous agents are at the frontier of language model (LM) applications research. Capable of independently acting and completing complex tasks through tool-use and multi-step reasoning, agent-based systems are succeeding mere instruction-following chatbots [1] across many application areas, including coding [2], web browsing [3], computer use [4] and virtual assistants [5]. Current LM agents operate in reactive paradigm: reactive agents perform actions in response to an explicit user request [6]. However, users are sometimes unaware of which actions need completing, and specifying needs to an assistant increases cognitive load. Proactive agents overcome these shortcomings by observing the actions user takes and the information she recieves to infer her goals. For example, proactive assistant, after observing users half-finished shopping list and text message from their roommate saying they are out of soap, adds soap to the shopping list without the users request; reactive assistant would not do so until the user asks. Recent work has begun exploring proactive assistants using LMs [7, 8, 9], however, all prior approaches suffer from the same key limitation: reliance on passive, out-of-loop users for evaluation. We submit that proactive agents may only be faithfully evaluated through interaction. Because user behavior is shaped by the behavior of an assistant, in the absence of real or simulated users interacting with the assistant, goal completion of the user-assisant system cannot be assessed. To dynamically and scalably evaluate proactive assistants for goal-oriented impact, we introduce the Proactive Agent Research Environment, Pare. Pare extends the prior Agent Research Environment Proactive Agent Research Environment Figure 1: Overview of the Pare framework architecture. Pare framework consists of an event-based environment that models Stateful App transitions. The user-environment interface exposes selective tools based on the current state St of the currently active app, and user actions result in app state transitions. Whereas the agent-environment interface exposes all tools as flat API structure to enable efficient information gathering and task execution by Proactive Assistants. (ARE) [10] by creating ecologically valid user simulator agents to interact with proactive assistants under test. Building these user simulator agents requires deep modifications to the existing environment. Unlike assistants which have unrestricted API access, real users must navigate the interfaces between and within an app to act and access information, as shown in Figure 2. To model this, the user simulator agents in Pare interact with special stateful app interfaces. User state is modeled by app-specific and global finite state machines (FSMs) which describe the interface logic real users would navigate. Rather than being able to make any API call at time, user agent must navigate the FSM to select function and populate its arguments (e.g. Figure 7), thereby simulating the interface navigation process human users must take. This asymmetry reflects real-world deployment, where user is restricted to actions enabled by the current context on the screen, whereas personal assistant on the same phone can directly access any functionality through backend APIs. An overview of the framework is presented in Figure 1. We use Pare to build Pare-Bench, benchmark of 143 diverse tasks designed to evaluate proactive assistants context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration capabilities across communication, productivity, scheduling, and lifestyle applications. Using Pare-Bench we evaluate seven language model-based proactive assistants. In summary, our contributions are as follows: Proactive Agent Research Environment (Pare): An asymmetric simulation framework where users navigate state-dependent actions while proactive agents access flat APIs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to explore an asymmetric FSM-based environment design for evaluating proactive assistants. Observe-Execute Proactive Architecture: two-phased agent design that separates the continuous monitoring from task execution, preserving the users autonomy. Pare-Bench: benchmark of 143 diverse tasks across four application domains, testing context observation, goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration. Proactive Agent Research Environment 2. Related Work LLMs as User Simulators Generative Agents [11] demonstrated that LLMs can simulate passable human behavior in social environments. This insight has been applied to agentic evaluation work, τ-bench [12] uses LLM-simulated users to test tool-agent interaction in customer service domains, while ToolSandbox [13] introduces stateful tool execution with built-in user simulator for conversational evaluation. τ2-bench [6] extends this further by allowing simulated users to also take actions, creating dual-control environment for technical support scenarios. However, none of the above benchmarks target mobile environments or model the screen-by-screen navigation characteristics of phone interfaces. Pare addresses this gap with FSM-based apps where simulated users navigate through state-dependent action spaces, mirroring real mobile interaction patterns. Moreover, while these advances have produced capable reactive agents, they share fundamental limitation that the user has to initiate the conversation, thus cannot be used as effective personal assistants. We now turn our attention to proactive agents, capable of observing user actions and shared environment to infer tasks for the user and complete them proactively. Mixed-Initiative Interfaces and Proactive Agents Researchers have long sought to study the problem of proactive personal assistants and mixed-initiative designs in the field of human-computer interaction. Maes [14] proposed interface agents as personal apprentices that learn user preferences through observation and provide assistance with email, scheduling, and information filtering. Horvitz [15] formalized the principles for mixed-initiative interface design, emphasizing that agent interventions must be calibrated to confidence and the cost of errors, while Shneiderman and Maes [16] highlighted the tension between direct user control and intelligent agent autonomy. Rhodes [17] introduces the Remembrance Agent, just-int-time information retrieval system that proactively surfaces relavant information based on the users current context. These foundational works established the core challenges that persist today in the age of LLM-based proactive agents. Recent works have started exploring the use of LLMs as proactive assistants. ProactiveAgent [7] proposes an LLM-driven gym where user activities and agent proposals are represented as natural language descriptions, but neither the user nor the agent execute any tool calls. Their generated events are textual and describe outcomes, but are not executable; as result, the gym cannot track state. ContextAgent [9] builds contextaware agent system for proactive assistance based on sensory data, but evaluates it on static dataset samples rather than an interactive simulation. ProAgent [8] extends this with on-demand sensory context, yet similarly lacks dynamic environment interaction. These approaches capture the high-level intent of proactive interaction, however, lack the grounded execution semantics that are necessary for realistic evaluation, i.e., the simulated users do not navigate through app screens, tools do not modify persistent state, and agents cannot observe the consequences of their actions in dyanmic environment. We argue that meaningful proactive agent evaluation requires an environment where users interact through state-dependent actions, as they would on real-world UI based system and agents execute actions that modify shared state. Pare bridges this gap by combining proactive goal inference with grounded execution in feature-rich stateful mobile environment. Simulated users navigate FSM-based apps through statedependent actions while proactive agents access APIs from all apps at once, which reflects the assymtery of real-world deployment. This design enabled evaluation of the complete proactive assistance loop, observing user actions, inferring goals, proposing interventions, and executing multi-step tasks. 3 Proactive Agent Research Environment 3. Proactive Agent Research Environment Pare is built on top of the Agent Research Environment (ARE) platform [10], which provides abstractions for building reactive agent environments with apps, scenarios, and verification. ARE agents receive instructions from simulated user, but this user cannot act on the environment directly. ARE agents are reactive, and do not infer user goals or propose actions, only performing as instructed. Without simulated users to interact with, we cannot evaluate proactive assistants. Pare extends ARE chiefly by implementing active user simulator agents inside the simulation environment. While crude user simulator could be implemented simply by prompting second agent to pretend to be user and take turns with the assistant, as shown in Figure 2, this approach would fail to model the actual constraints users face as opposed to assistants. Pare facilitates this simulation of active users by implementing stateful agent interfaces which simulated users can use, as well as asymmetrical message passing and observation APIs to model how users and assistants interact. 3.1 Formal Definition proactive agent works collaboratively with user within shared environment state space and action space A. Both the user and agent make observations in observation space O. We formalize this as Stackelberg POMDP [18, 19] (detailed in Appendix D). The user has some set of goals GU which are not explicitly stated, and takes series of actions AU toward them. The proactive agent attempts to infer user goals from their actions and the evolving state of the environment, and then proposes plans = {ai A} to the user. If the user believes plan will accomplish one of their goals, they accept the plan and the agent performs its actions. The agent and the user follow Stackelberg turn-taking structure [20], where at time step the user first acts, then the agent develops its plan, function of its own prior actions and its observations of the users prior actions and the current environment state St, and the past sequence of environment events Et. pt = fθ(OAt1, AAt1, AUt1, Et) (1) At most time steps, no action is proposed, pt = . All proposed plans are part of the agents plan set PA. The binary function ACCEPTU(pi, GU) is true if the user believes plan pi will help achieve one or more of their goals, in which case the plans actions are executed and added to the agent action set AA. ACCEPTU(pi) = AAt = AAt1 {ai pt} (2) After both the user and assistant stop, if some goal gi in GU is successfully fulfilled following the combined actions of the user and agent, we say the binary indicator function SUCCEED(gi, AU AA, Sfinal) = 1 is true. The goal of proactive agent is to jointly maximize the goal success rate RSucceed and the plan acceptance rate RAccept as follows: RAccept = 1 PA piPA ACCEPTU(pi, GU) RSucceed = 1 GU giGU SUCCEED(gi, AU AA, Sfinal) (3) (4) Proactive Agent Research Environment 3.2 User and assistant action interfaces Pare environment includes user agent and assistant agent which interact with shared set of apps and recieve environment-level messages corresponding to external events. The user moves first, and then the agent and user alternate taking actions (or doing nothing) until maximum number of turns is reached or completion signal from the environment is provided. The user agent in Pare is modified version of the ReAct agent [21] provided by ARE, which has restricted API access to faithfully model user. In particular, the model only has access to limited set of actions at any time, governed by its current user state (subsection 3.3), and it has limited observation compared to the assistant (subsection 3.5). In principle, the user and the assistant can perform identical operations on the underlying database (and thus the global environment state S), but through different interfaces: the assistant makes direct API calls, while the user must traverse app states to populate arguments and invoke functions. These user state navigation actions represent distinct actions in A, which can be observed by the assistant. core system app exposes navigation tools, such as open, switch, and, close app to the user and utility functions such as current_time to both the user and the assistant. Finally, an AgentUserInterface app is required to provide an interface between the assistant and the user agents. Through which the agent can access send_message_to_user to propose plan, and the user can respond with accept_proposal and reject_proposal. 3.3 User state and transitions We represent the user state space as finite state machine (FSM). Each application has its own FSM which exposes user-only tools as an interface between the user simulator and the underlying ARE APIs. Each state represents screen in the app, and transitions represent behaviors such as filling forms in fields, navigating screens, and submitting requests. For example, Figure 2 shows how sending message in existing agent frameworks requires only two API calls (search conversation, send message), while in Pare the user must navigate through realistic sequence of screens: opening the Messages app, searching for the conversation, opening it, and then sending the message. We show the complete FSM state diagram for the Email app in Figure 7. In addition to app-level user state, environment-level user state, i.e. which app is currently active, and which apps are running in the background, is also tracked. The environment orchestrates app interactions and maintains the global navigation state. The user has access to the stack of previously opened background apps and can return to them using app navigation commands from the environment-level system app. Thus, all user actions are user FSM state transitions. 3.4 Environment events Depending on the scenario, Pare environments contain events which generate updates to the environment database as well as broadcast messages to the user and assistant. Events can be scheduled at absolute timestamps or relative to other events, enabling dynamic scenarios where extenal such as colleague replying to an email can be simulated, allowing scenarios spanning hours of simulated time to execute in minutes. These events Et condition the agent and users choice of actions at timestep as shown in Equation 1. 5 Proactive Agent Research Environment Figure 2: Comparison of tool call chains for sending message. Non-FSM agent frameworks allow direct API calls (left), while Pares FSM-based design requires the user to navigate through sequential app screens (right), matching how real users interact with mobile apps. 3.5 User and assistant observation asymmetry The user and the assistant agent access different information to select their next action. Equation 1 shows that the agents choice of plan proposal (and actions) is function of its previous observations OAt1 , the actions of itself and the user, AAt1 , and prior environment events Et. However, reflecting the limited observability that real users have into the internals of the system, the user agent only accesses its own past actions and observations, and cannot see the agents actions: , AUt1 AUt = fU(OUt1, AUt1, Et) (5) Pare routes event notifications to the appropriate recipient in line with AREs notification system. For example, user actions are forwarded to the proactive agent as observations, agent messages are delivered to the user for acceptance or rejection, and environment events such as incoming emails or calendar reminders are broadcasted to both. The level of verbosity at which event notifications are added to the event stack is configurable. Users receive truncated notifications that mirror real mobile phone behavior, for instance, new email notification displays the sender, subject line and truncated content. In contrast, proactive agents receive the full serialized content. 3.6 Scenarios Scenarios define an evaluation task for proactive agents. Each scenario specifies an initial environment state, sequence of initial events or triggers for proactive agents, and validation criteria for success. Following AREs scenario abstraction, Pare scenarios are divided into three components, app initialization, event flow construction, and oracle validation. App initialization populates the environment with scenario-specific data such as contacts, calendar events, email threads etc., establishing the context. The event flow defines scheduled environment events, and oracle events that specify expected proactive behavior. To simulate real-world conditions, Pare inherits AREs configurable distractor notifications and tool failure simulation, enabling scenarios with varying levels of difficulty. Proactive Agent Research Environment 4. User Simulator and Proactive Assistant Pare orchestrates two LLM-based agents, and active user simulator and proactive assistant that interact through Stackelberg turn-based loop [20, 13], where the user acts first and the assistant observes and takes actions. Both agents are built on AREs BaseAgent [10] and follow the Reasoning Action Observation cycle introduced in ReAct [21]. All agents in Pare can be configured to take multiple actions during their turn, and this is treated as simulation parameter. In the following sections, we discuss the implementation details of the Active User Simulator and Proactive Assistant. 4.1 User Simulator Agent As discussed in subsection 3.2, the user simulator has restricted access to the tools based on the current app state. This constraint is modeled through the user-environment interface and doesnt change the implementation of the ReAct agent. At each turn, the agent receives list of available apps, current state, and available tools dynamically from the user-environment interface Figure 1 based on the currently active app and navigation state. The agent can explore the environment in an open-ended fashion or respond to incoming notifications, similar to Park et al. [11]. Beyond navigating apps, the user agent evaluates proposals from the proactive agent. We model the accept_proposal and reject_proposal as context-dependent tools that are only exposed to the user simulator when there is an assistant proposal awaiting review. The user simulator model is instructed to be strict about acceptance, rejecting vague proposals, misunderstanding the intent or context, or requiring capabilities beyond available apps. 4.2 Proactive Assistant We implement the Proactive Assistant as combination of two separate sub-agents: Observe and Execute. This architecture design is motivated by the plan-then-execute pattern, which is common in agentic systems [22]. The assistant system has three modes: observe, awaiting confirmation, and execute, with awaiting confirmation being transient state where the assistant is waiting for the user to accept proposal. Observe Mode In observe mode, the agent monitors user actions and environment notifications to identify proactive assistance opportunities. The assistant only has access to read-only tools for information gathering, and two control actions: wait to continue observing, or send_message_to_user to propose assistance. Using either control action results in the termination of the assistants turn. If the assistants proposal is accepted by the user, the assistant transitions to execution mode with the proposed goal as the initial task. Execute Mode The executor has access to the complete flat API across all apps included in the scenario, which enables it to complete tasks that span multiple applications. The executor works autonomously, messaging the user only upon completion or if the task proves impossible. After execution, the assistant returns to observe mode, ready to identify the next proactive assistance opportunity. Similarly, if the user rejects proposal, the agent returns to the observe mode without invoking the executor. 7 Proactive Agent Research Environment 5. Pare-Bench Building on the Pare framework, we create Pare-Bench, benchmark of 143 scenarios designed to evaluate proactive agents across diverse contexts and apps. While scenarios can be authored manually, this approach does not scale to the volume required for comprehensive evaluation. Similar to recent works that employ LLMs to generate diverse tasks for LLM training and evaluation, we design an LLM-based scenario generation agent [23, 24] that produces candidate scenarios. Scenario generation can be broken down into four phases: story generation, initial app data population, building events flow, and validation. Once our scenario generation agent has generated candidate scenarios, each scenario is verified by the authors of this work to ensure story coherence, correctness of the task execution validation criteria, and ensure the content of the scenario events is realistic. We discuss the complete Scenario Generation Pipeline in Appendix E. Pare-Bench scenarios use all the apps specified in Table 5. Since PASAgentUserInterface and System apps provide the core interface for assistant-user communication and inter-app navigation for the user simulator, all scenarios include these core applications. The distribution of applications used across the benchmark is shown in Figure 6. As discussed in subsection 3.6, we can increase the benchmark diversity by simulating tool failures and spurious notifications such as promotional or spam emails. Fowllowing ARE [10], noise events are scheduled using Poisson process and interleaved with scenario events. By varying the noise density, we can evaluate whether assistant models can distinguish signal from noise. 6. Experiments We evaluate seven LLMs as proactive assistants on Pare-Bench: four closed-source models (Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro, Gemini 3 Flash) and three open-weights models (Qwen 3 4B, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B). We argue that proactive assistants should ideally be deployed as on-device models given the privacy implications of continuously observing user actions. We include frontier models to establish performance ceiling and smaller models to evaluate the feasibility of on-device deployment. Table 1: Model performance metrics on Pare-Bench across 4 runs. We report standard error (). Proposal Rate follows lower is better, since too many proposals can annoy the user. The best performing model is highlighted in bold for each metric. Metrics As defined in subsection 3.1, we evaluate proactive assistants on Acceptance Rate (RAccept) and Task Execution Success (RSucceed). Since proactive tasks can exhibit high variance across runs, we report 8 Proactive Agent Research Environment success using three variants: Success@k checks if the assistant succeeds in at least one of runs, Success^k requires success across all runs and captures model reliability, and Success Rate calculates the average across all runs. We also track Proposal Rate, the proportion of turns in which the assistant proposed task. high proposal rate paired with low acceptance suggests the assistant is too eager and fails to gather sufficient context before intervening. We report the average number of read-only actions per scenario as proxy for information gathering by the assistant. Configuration We use GPT-5-mini as the user simulation model. The simulation runs for maximum of 10 turns. The user agent is configured to run 1 iteration per turn, while the proactive agent receives 5 iterations for observe mode and 10 iterations for execute mode. We use symmetric setup where the same LLM powers both the observe and execute agents. 6.1 Results Table 1 presents results across all seven models. We find that Gemini 3 Flash and Claude 4.5 Sonnet perform comparably at the top, with success rates of 42.1% and 42.0% respectively. Among the open-weights models, Qwen 3 4B Instruct leads across all metrics, while Gemma 3 4B Instruct struggles considerably at only 3.0% success rate. Consistency The gap between Success@4 and Success^4 reveals how consistent models are across repeated runs. Claude drops from 60.8% to 18.2% (a 3.3 reduction), but Llama exhibits 17.0 reduction from 23.8% to 1.4%. This indicates that smaller models are not only less capable but far less consistent. Figure 3: Proposal Rate, Acceptance rate, and Execution Success of different models with respect to tool failure probability evaluation across 4 runs. Proposal Quality and Efficiency Acceptance rate ranges from 78.2% for Claude to 17.6% for Gemma. Claude combines the lowest proposal rate (12.8%) with the highest acceptance rate, indicating that it proposes only when it is confident. Gemini 3 Flash achieves comparable success rate to Claude (42.1% vs 42.0%), but with higher proposal rate (19.1%) and lower acceptance (67.1%), which suggests that Gemini requires more proposals and more user interaction to achieve similar outcomes. GPT-5 shows the highest proposal rate (28.1%) paired with moderate acceptance (70.2%), suggesting overconfidence in its goal inferences. Gemma maintains the lowest proposal rate (14.2%) despite its poor acceptance (17.6%), Proactive Agent Research Environment Figure 4: Proposal Rate, Acceptance rate, and Execution Success of different models with respect to environment event noise evaluation across 4 runs. which indicates that it is both passive and inaccurate when it does propose. We present finer-grained analysis of proposal decision patterns in Appendix B. Information Gathering and Execution We observe correlation between read actions and overall performance. The top performing models, Gemini 3 Flash (21.7 actions), GPT-5 (20.6), and Claude (20.2), perform approximately 20% more information gathering than Qwen and Llama (approximately 16.7), and nearly twice as many as Gemma (8.8). This suggests that sufficient environmental observation is prerequisite for accurate goal inference. Interestingly, Qwen shows relatively high acceptance rate (63.7%) but much lower success rate (18.5%), which suggests that for smaller models, execution rather than goal inference is the primary bottleneck. 6.2 Robustness under Environment Noise Tool Failure We simulate tool failures with probability 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 (Figure 3). Proposal and acceptance rates remain stable across all failure probabilities. Success rates, however, reveal meaningful differences: Claude maintains 40-45% even at 40% failure probability, while Llama drops from 20% to 8% and Qwen remains stable at 18-20%. Environment Noise We inject spurious notifications at 2, 4, and 6 events per minute to test whether models can distinguish relevant events from noise (Figure 4). Proposal and acceptance rates remain stable across noise levels for all models. We find that Claude maintains consistent success rates across all noise densities, while Gemini 3 Flash and GPT-5 degrade noticeably at higher noise. Among smaller models, Qwen remains stable while Llama degrades, and Gemma stays near zero throughout. This suggests that robustness to noisy environments varies considerably across models regardless of scale. We further study the effect of user model choice on benchmark results in Appendix C. 7. Conclusion We introduce Pare, framework for evaluating proactive assistants through active user simulation with FSMbased stateful app interfaces, Pare-Bench, benchmark of 143 tasks testing goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration, and the Observe-Execute agent architecture. Our experiments show 10 Proactive Agent Research Environment that even the best frontier models achieve only 42% success rate, and that for smaller models execution is the primary bottleneck, which the Observe-Execute design can partially address. We discuss limitations and future work in Appendix A.

sections.title: Impact Statement
sections.content: This work introduces Pare, framework and benchmark for proactive AI assistants, advancing research in goal inference, intervention timing, and multi-app orchestration. We strongly believe that since Proactive agents require observing user actions, they should use models deployed on edge devices which eliminates the need for uploading user data to external servers. Moreover, we make conscious decision of modeling user actions as APIs. This API-level abstraction provides natural privacy boundaryagents observe what actions occur, not everything visible on screen. With advent of Proactive Assistants, another concern is the loss of autonomy of the human users. We believe proactive assistants can be valuable if developed with human as the sole point of control. This sets up the motivation for our Observe-then-Execute architecture which forces the agent to ask permission before executing any task. We release Pare framework and Pare-Bench to enable reproducible research and encourage the community to build capable yet privacy-preserving proactive assistants.

URL https://arxiv.org/abs/ 2402.01030. A. Limitations and Future Work Limitations Pare models app interactions as tool calls rather than visual screen interactions. While this is deliberate design choice that provides natural privacy boundary and enables scalable evaluation, it means we do not test the visual grounding capabilities that would be required in real multimodal deployment. Our user simulation is LM-based, and while our user model study (Appendix C) shows that relative rankings of proactive assistants are preserved across different user models, simulated users may not capture all nuances of real human behavior such as fatigue, emotional state, and multitasking patterns. Moreover, our user simulation does not model individual differences in user personality and trust levels. In practice, some users are more trusting and would accept proposals without verifying the underlying content, while others actively reject proposals if the intervention timing does not match their preferences. Modeling this personalization through theory-of-mind approaches [25] remains an open challenge. 13 Proactive Agent Research Environment In our main experiments, we use symmetric setup where the same model powers both the observe and execute agents. The intended deployment pattern is asymmetric, with smaller on-device model for continuous observation and larger model invoked for execution, but we do not evaluate this configuration. While Pare-Bench includes 143 scenarios spanning multiple apps and domains, the benchmark may not cover all real-world proactive assistance situations, such as long-horizon tasks spanning multiple days or tasks requiring world knowledge beyond the app ecosystem. Future Work natural next step is to evaluate asymmetric observe-execute configurations. Our finding that execution is the primary bottleneck for smaller models motivates pairing small on-device observation model with larger execution model. For example, quantized 4B parameter model could run continuously on-device for observation, while cloud-hosted model is invoked only when the user accepts proposal, keeping user data on-device until explicit consent is given. Expanding the app ecosystem with more complex apps and richer inter-app dependencies would enable more realistic multi-app orchestration scenarios. For instance, adding banking app with transaction capabilities would introduce safety-critical actions where the cost of incorrect proactive intervention is significantly higher, testing whether agents can calibrate their proposal confidence accordingly. The proactive assistance task can be naturally modeled as POMDP, where the hidden state includes the users latent goals and the agent must select actions based on partial observations. The acceptance and success metrics defined in subsection 3.1 can serve as reward signals for training proactive agents using reinforcement learning, potentially closing the gap between frontier and smaller models. Incorporating UI screenshots alongside API-based interactions would enable evaluation of multimodal proactive agents. This would bridge the gap between our API-level abstraction and real-world deployment, where agents must process visual context from the users screen to infer goals and timing for intervention. B. Proposal Decision Analysis The binary accept/reject analysis in the main experiments does not capture the full picture of user-agent interaction. We analyze the trajectories generated by the main experiment run (GPT-5-mini as user model, 4 runs per scenario) and categorize user responses into three decisions: accept, reject, and gather context. In the gather context case, the user ignores the proposal and continues exploring the environment to gather more information on their own before deciding. For example, when the user receives truncated notification about new email, the proactive assistant may propose to schedule meeting based on the email. The user, having only seen the truncated notification, may choose to ignore the proposal and navigate to the email app to read the full content before deciding. If instead the assistant had waited for the user to read the email first, the user would have had full context and the proposal would have higher chance of direct acceptance. high gather context rate indicates that the assistants intervention timing is premature. User Simulator Ablation To understand how user model choice affects benchmark results, we vary the user simulation model while keeping the proactive assistant models fixed. We compare three user models: GPT-5-mini (our default from the main experiments), Claude 4.5 Sonnet, and Qwen 3 4B. Each user model is paired with four proactive assistants (Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-5, Gemini 3 Flash, and Qwen 3 4B) on the ablation split (50 scenarios, 4 runs each). Results are shown in Table 4. User Model Sensitivity Since the proactive agent observes and reacts to user actions, the user model can indirectly affect the proactive assistants scenario success rates. We find that Claude as the user is the strictest evaluator: acceptance rates drop to the 23-49% range and success rates compress to 13-26% across all proactive models. Qwen as the user is the most permissive, with acceptance rates uniformly high (74-80%) across all proactive models, yet success rates still vary widely (11-44%). GPT-5-mini sits in between, with sufficient spread in both acceptance and success rates to discriminate between models. This validates our choice of GPT-5-mini as the default user model for the main experiments. Ranking Stability Despite large shifts in absolute numbers, we find that the relative ordering of proactive assistants is preserved across all three user models. We observe no same-model bias: Claude achieves the highest acceptance rate regardless of which model powers the user (49.5% with Claude user, 79.8% with GPT-5-mini, 80.0% with Qwen). Proposal rate patterns are also largely intrinsic to each proactive model, with Claude consistently proposing the least and GPT-5 the most, though absolute values shift slightly with different user models. Proactive Agent Research Environment Observation Behavior We observe that read actions increase significantly with Qwen as the user (20-26 range) compared to other user models (15-20). This suggests that user behavior patterns influence how much the proactive agent explores the environment. Even with high read actions and the most permissive user, Qwen as proactive assistant achieves 0% Pass^4, reinforcing that information gathering alone does not compensate for poor execution capability. D. Stackelberg POMDP Formulation We formalize Pare as Stackelberg POMDP [18, 19] defined by the tuple = , S, {Ai}iN , T, R, {Oi}iN , I, where = {U, A} denotes the user (leader) and the proactive agent (follower). The user drives the interaction by taking actions first, and the proactive agent observes user behavior before deciding whether to intervene. Each component of the tuple is detailed below with examples drawn from the mobile environment. State space (S) The global state is decomposed as = Sapp Sglobal Sdb Shistory. Sapp represents the current screen within the active app; for example, in the Contacts app this could be the contacts list, specific contacts detail view, or the edit screen for that contact. Sglobal tracks which app is currently in the foreground. Sdb captures the persistent data across all apps, such as contacts, emails, calendar events, and shopping cart items. Shistory maintains bounded view of past events for each agent, including the navigation stack, recent actions, and notifications. Action spaces (Ai) The users available actions are state-dependent: AU : Sapp Sglobal 2Aall . For example, from the contacts list the user can search contacts, open contact, or create new one, but cannot edit contact without first navigating to the detail view. When proposal from the agent is pending, the users action space is augmented with accept_proposal and reject_proposal. The proactive agents action space is state-independent: AA = Aread Apropose {wait}, where Aread includes read-only queries across all apps, Apropose allows the agent to propose task to the user, and wait continues observation without intervention. Figure 5: Scenario generation agent pipeline. Given Pare codebase and target apps, the agent generates scenarios through four stages: description, data population, event flow, and validation. Each stage (except description) runs in oracle mode for execution-based validation, with failed attempts looping back for retry. The uniqueness check ensures no duplicate scenarios. 17 Proactive Agent Research Environment Observation spaces (Oi) The users observation OU(s) is function of the current state only, and includes the current screen, available tools, any pending agent proposal, and truncated environment notifications. For example, new email notification shows only the sender and subject line, not the full content. The agents observation OA(s, aU) is function of both the state and the users action, reflecting the Stackelberg structure. The agent observes the users executed actions, receives full environment notifications, and sees the users response to any pending proposal. Transition function (T) : AU AA defines the state dynamics. The base model is deterministic: for example, the user calling open_contact("C001") from the contacts list transitions Sapp from ContactsList to ContactDetail("C001"). stochastic extension supports configurable tool failure probability for robustness experiments (Figure 3). Reward function (R) Pare uses dual reward structure: = (RSucceed, RAccept), corresponding to the metrics defined in subsection 3.1. RSucceed is terminal binary reward that equals 1 if the users goals GU are fulfilled in the final environment state, as verified by the scenario oracle. RAccept is per-step reward: +1 for accepted proposals, 1 for rejected proposals, and 0 otherwise. Instruction space (I) = IU IA specifies task instructions for each agent at the start of an episode. The user receives natural language goal description, for example You received an email about meeting with Alice. Schedule it on your calendar. The agent receives generic proactive assistance instructions that describe its role as an observer and helper. Episode dynamics Each turn follows the Stackelberg structure: the user acts first, the agent observes the users action, then the agent acts. The environment updates the state and computes rewards after both agents have acted. Episodes terminate when the maximum number of turns Tmax is reached or the environment signals completion. E. Scenario Generation Pipeline E.1 Scenario Generation Agent As discussed in subsection 3.6, scenario in Pare consists of three components: initial app states, environment event flow and validation. Following this abstraction, we design our scenario generation agent in four-stage pipeline (Figure 5), scenario description generation, initial app state population, building events flow and validation function. We use code generation agent based on Claude-Agent-SDK1, which receives the Pare codebase in read-only context and selection of target apps that it has to include in the scenario. In the first stage, the agent generates natural language description of the scenario, which is checked against existing scenario description for uniqueness using LLM-as-a-judge [26, 27]. 1https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python 18 Proactive Agent Research Environment Based on this description, in the second stage, the scenario generates the init_and_populate_apps function which populates the initial app states with seed data required for the scenario. The third stage is responsible for generating the environment event flow along with oracle user and agent actions by implementing the build_event_flow function. Finally, the agent generates the validation conditions for each scenario and checks the required actions for scenario completion. Upon generating functions for each stage two through four, we run the scenario in the oracle mode (i.e. only run the scenario with oracle and environment events) to check for syntax and to ensure that the scenario executes correctly. If the check fails, the agent is given the execution feedback and asked to retry [28] This execution driven approach ensures that generated scenarios are syntactically correct. To ensure high quality scenario generation, human verifies the generated scenario for semantic coherence. F. G. Scenario Example We show an example of the proactive assistance scenario in Pare in Figure 8. In this scenario, the system is triggered by an external email event related to the users apartment search budget. Once the email arrives, the environment processes it through the event loop and injects notifications into both the user-facing context and the assistant-facing context. The user receives truncated notification and can inspect the email content through the mailbox interface, while the proactive assistant observes the complete email and interprets it as constraint update on the apartment search task. After observing the event, the assistant queries the apartment application to retrieve the users saved apartments and evaluates them against the newly inferred budget constraint. Through this analysis, the assistant identifies several saved apartments whose prices exceed the specified monthly budget. Instead of directly modifying the users saved list, the assistant summarizes the findings and proactively generates proposal, explicitly listing the apartments that violate the constraint and the expected outcome if they are removed. 23 Proactive Agent Research Environment Figure 8: Sequence diagram illustrating proactive agent scenario in the Proactive Agent Research Environment. The assistant then communicates this proposal to the user and transitions into waiting-for-confirmation state. Only after receiving an explicit approval message from the user does the assistant execute the corresponding state-altering actions to remove the over-budget apartments. These actions result in validated state transitions within the apartment application, leaving only budget-compliant options in the saved list. This scenario concretely illustrates how PARE enables proactive, user-in-the-loop assistance by grounding agent reasoning in observable events, enforcing user confirmation before execution, and ensuring transparent state evolution.
